function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
As of March 31, 2026, SpaceX disclosed approximately 9,600 broadband satellites supporting 10.3M Starlink subscribers and approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites supporting 7.4M monthly unique satellite-to-mobile devices. Calculate the users-per-satellite ratio for each network and explain the utilization implications.

steps.reasoning_content: The user is asking me to calculate users-per-satellite ratios for two SpaceX networks (Starlink broadband and V1 Mobile) and explain utilization implications. Let me first verify the figures cited from the SpaceX S-1 filing by parsing and retrieving the relevant information.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Good, the filing has been parsed. Let me now retrieve information about the Starlink broadband satellites and subscribers, as well as the V1 Mobile satellites and devices.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, retrieve the number of broadband satellites in orbit and the number of Starlink subscribers as of March 31, 2026, or the most recent date disclosed. Also retrieve the number of V1 Mobile satellites and monthly unique satellite-to-mobile devices. Look for satellite counts and subscriber/user metrics in the business overview or Starlink segment discussion.

results.content:

*   **Broadband Satellites in Orbit:** Approximately **9,000 broadband satellites** (this figure is specifically cited in the context of the consumer broadband constellation).
*   **Total Starlink Broadband & Mobile Satellites:** The overall constellation consists of approximately **9,600 satellites**, which includes both broadband and mobile satellites.
*   **Starlink Subscribers:** Approximately **10.3 million** subscribers.
*   **V1 Mobile Satellites:** Approximately **650** satellites dedicated to the satellite-to-mobile constellation.
*   **Monthly Unique Satellite-to-Mobile Devices:** Approximately **7.4 million** unique devices accessing satellite-to-mobile services.

**Summary Table:**

| Metric | Value | As of Date |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Broadband Satellites** | ~9,000 | March 31, 2026 |
| **Total Satellites (Broadband & Mobile)**| ~9,600 | March 31, 2026 |
| **Starlink Subscribers** | ~10.3 million | March 31, 2026 |
| **V1 Mobile Satellites** | ~650 | March 31, 2026 |
| **Monthly Unique Satellite-to-Mobile Devices** | ~7.4 million | March 31, 2026 |

The question states "approximately 9,600 broadband satellites" but the filing seems to indicate that 9,600 is the total constellation (broadband + mobile), with broadband being ~9,000. Actually, looking at the question again: "SpaceX disclosed approximately 9,600 broadband satellites supporting 10.3M Starlink subscribers and approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites supporting 7.4M monthly unique satellite-to-mobile devices."

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, retrieve the exact text describing the Starlink constellation size. Specifically, what is the breakdown between broadband satellites and V1 Mobile satellites? Is the 9,600 figure the total constellation or just broadband? Look for any passage that discusses the total number of satellites in orbit and how they are categorized between broadband and mobile.

results.content:
# Starlink Constellation Size Breakdown

Based on the provided text chunks, here is what can be pieced together:

## Total Constellation
The texts consistently reference **approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites** in Low-Earth Orbit as of March 31, 2026. This 9,600 figure is explicitly described as encompassing **both** broadband and mobile satellites — it is the total combined constellation.

## Mobile (V1 Mobile) Satellites
The text provides a clear growth trajectory for the mobile constellation:
- **January 2025:** approximately **360** V1 Mobile satellites
- **March 31, 2026:** approximately **650** V1 Mobile satellites (described as "dedicated mobile satellites")

## Broadband Satellites
The text does not give a single explicit number for broadband-only satellites in most passages, but one chunk states:

> *"Starlink Consumer Broadband is enabled by the largest satellite constellation in human history with approximately **9,000 broadband satellites** as of March 31, 2026"*

## Implied Breakdown

| Category | Approximate Count |
|----------|------------------|
| **Total** | ~9,600 |
| **Broadband** | ~9,000 |
| **Mobile (V1)** | ~650 |
| **Combined** | ~9,650 |

The slight arithmetic difference (~9,650 vs. ~9,600) likely reflects rounding in the disclosures. The majority of the broadband constellation is composed of **V2 Mini satellites**.

## Key Caveat
The texts note that broadband and mobile satellites are **distinct systems** with different performance requirements and technical specifications, even though they share a similar "V" naming convention.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have the precise breakdown. Let me calculate the users-per-satellite ratios:

1. **Starlink Broadband**: ~9,000 broadband satellites, ~10.3M subscribers
   - Users per satellite = 10,300,000 / 9,000 = ~1,144.44

2. **V1 Mobile**: ~650 V1 Mobile satellites, ~7.4M monthly unique devices
   - Users per satellite = 7,400,000 / 650 = ~11,384.62

Wait, the question says "approximately 9,600 broadband satellites" but the filing actually says ~9,000 broadband satellites and ~9,600 total (broadband + mobile). I should address this discrepancy. The question appears to conflate the 9,600 total constellation figure with "broadband satellites." I should use the filing's actual numbers.

Let me do the calculations.
So for Starlink Broadband: ~1,144.44 subscribers per satellite.

So for V1 Mobile: ~11,384.62 monthly unique devices per satellite.

Now let me also calculate if we used the question's stated 9,600 broadband figure (even though the filing says ~9,000 broadband):
Now let me also retrieve some additional context about the utilization implications - capacity, congestion, and growth plans.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, retrieve information about Starlink network capacity, congestion management, bandwidth per user, satellite throughput capacity, and any discussion of utilization rates or network load. Also look for information about planned satellite launches, constellation expansion plans, and how capacity growth compares to subscriber growth. Include any discussion of quality of service, average speeds, or latency metrics.

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from SpaceX's S-1 prospectus, here is a structured summary of information regarding Starlink's network capacity, performance metrics, and growth plans.

### **Current Network Capacity & Scale (as of March 31, 2026)**
*   **Constellation Size:** Approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites in Low-Earth Orbit (LEO), representing about 75% of all active maneuverable satellites in orbit.
*   **Cumulative Downlink Capacity:** The constellation supports over 700 Tbps of cumulative downlink capacity.
*   **Subscribers:** Approximately 10.3 million Starlink Subscribers across 164 countries, territories, and markets.
*   **Mobile Users:** Approximately 7.4 million monthly unique devices using satellite-to-mobile services across ~30 countries.

### **Per-Satellite Throughput & Technology**
*   **Current Satellites:** The majority of the constellation consists of second-generation, higher-throughput **V2 Mini satellites**.
*   **Next-Generation (V3) Satellites:** Scheduled to begin deployment in the second half of 2026, each V3 satellite is designed to offer **1 Tbps (1,000 Gbps) of downlink capacity**.
*   **Deployment Efficiency:** A single Starship launch will be capable of deploying up to **60 V3 satellites**, representing a potential **twenty-fold increase in downlink capacity deployed per launch** compared to a Falcon 9 launch.

### **Performance Metrics (Quality of Service)**
*   **Latency:** Median latency of approximately **25 milliseconds**.
*   **Download Speed:** Provides "fiber-like" download speeds at a median of **225 Mbps during peak hours** for residential users. The technology is capable of providing speeds exceeding **400 Mbps** with round-trip latencies as low as **21 milliseconds**.
*   **Coverage:** The service has the technological capability to provide connectivity everywhere on Earth, including the poles.

### **Congestion Management & Bandwidth Per User**
*   The documents do not detail specific congestion management protocols or hard caps on bandwidth per user. Instead, they emphasize that **increasing the total capacity of the constellation** is the primary method for supporting more customers and improving service during peak periods.
*   **Average Revenue Per User (ARPU):** Monthly Starlink Subscriber ARPU decreased from **$91 in 2024** to **$81 in 2025**, and from **$86 in Q1 2025** to **$66 in Q1 2026**. This decline is attributed to international expansion and the addition of lower-priced plans. The company expects this trend to continue but be offset by scale and cost reductions.

### **Utilization Rates & Network Load**
*   Specific utilization rates or current network load percentages are not provided. The focus is on **installed capacity**.
*   A key metric for the AI segment is **"Nameplate Compute Draw,"** which reached **1.0 gigawatt** as of March 2026. This metric measures installed GPU power draw in data centers and is separate from satellite network utilization.

### **Planned Capacity Growth & Expansion Plans**
1.  **Constellation Expansion:**
    *   Continue deploying **V2 Mini satellites**.
    *   Begin deploying **next-generation V3 satellites** (1 Tbps each) using Starship in H2 2026.
2.  **Mobile Constellation (Starlink Mobile Gen2):**
    *   Expanding from ~650 dedicated **V1 Mobile satellites**.
    *   Plan to deploy **next-generation V2 Mobile satellites in 2027**.
    *   Spectrum acquisition from EchoStar (65 MHz in the US, plus global MSS licenses) expected to close in November 2027 to enable "orders of magnitude" increased capacity.
3.  **Launch Vehicle:**
    *   **Starship** is critical for the rapid and cost-effective deployment of both V3 broadband and V2 Mobile satellites, significantly increasing per-launch capacity.
4.  **Growth Strategy:**
    *   The goal is to **onboard new users while improving service** for existing ones by materially increasing constellation capacity.
    *   For mobile services, the aim is to "substantially reducing mobile 'dead zones' worldwide" with future 5G connectivity to unmodified phones.

### **Enterprise & Government Capacity**
*   **Enterprise Solutions:** Provide high-speed, low-latency, and reliable internet for businesses across various industries (aviation, maritime, etc.). Contracts are based on subscriptions, data consumption, capacity, or other pricing models.
*   **Government Solutions (Starshield):** A separate, secure satellite network for government customers, providing high-speed, resilient connectivity.

**In summary,** the prospectus highlights that Starlink's network capacity is being aggressively scaled through next-generation satellites and Starship launches to support rapid subscriber growth, improve performance, and enter new markets (mobile broadband). Key metrics show strong current performance (speed, latency), with a strategic focus on increasing total capacity rather than disclosing granular utilization data.

2. The different service models (broadband vs. direct-to-device)
3. The cumulative capacity of 700 Tbps across the constellation
4. The planned V3 satellite upgrades (1 Tbps each)
5. The ARPU trends suggesting lower-ARPU markets driving growth

arguments.final_result:
## Users-Per-Satellite Ratio Analysis

### Important Filing Clarification

The question states "approximately 9,600 broadband satellites," but the SpaceX S-1 filing (dated May 20, 2026) actually distinguishes the constellation as follows as of March 31, 2026:

| Category | Satellites | Users | 
|---|---|---|
| **Starlink Broadband** | ~9,000 | ~10.3M subscribers |
| **V1 Mobile (satellite-to-mobile)** | ~650 | ~7.4M monthly unique devices |
| **Total Constellation** | ~9,600 (combined) | — |

The 9,600 figure is the **total combined constellation** (broadband + mobile), not broadband alone. The calculations below use the filing's actual category-level figures.

---

### Users-Per-Satellite Calculations

**Starlink Broadband:**

$$\frac{10{,}300{,}000 \text{ subscribers}}{9{,}000 \text{ satellites}} = 1{,}144.44 \text{ subscribers per satellite}$$

**V1 Mobile (Satellite-to-Mobile):**

$$\frac{7{,}400{,}000 \text{ monthly unique devices}}{650 \text{ satellites}} = 11{,}384.62 \text{ devices per satellite}$$

| Network | Users/Satellite Ratio |
|---|---|
| Starlink Broadband | **~1,144** subscribers per satellite |
| V1 Mobile | **~11,385** devices per satellite |

The V1 Mobile network serves roughly **10× more users per satellite** than the broadband network.

---

### Utilization Implications

**1. Fundamentally Different Service Models Explain the Ratio Gap**

The ~10:1 disparity does not necessarily indicate that mobile satellites are more "stressed." The two networks deliver qualitatively different services:

- **Broadband**: Each satellite must deliver sustained, high-bandwidth connections (median 225 Mbps download at peak hours) to fixed terminals with SLA-like expectations. Capacity per user is substantial and relatively continuous.
- **V1 Mobile**: Serves unmodified smartphones on a best-effort, intermittent basis for low-bandwidth tasks (messaging, SOS, basic data). Individual sessions consume far less capacity per device. The 7.4M figure counts *monthly unique devices*, not concurrent connections—many devices may only ping the network briefly.

**2. Aggregate Constellation Capacity**

The filing discloses the constellation supports **over 700 Tbps of cumulative downlink capacity**. This implies approximately:
- ~78 Gbps per satellite on average across the entire ~9,000-satellite broadband fleet
- This is consistent with the V2 Mini satellite technology that comprises the majority of the current constellation

At the broadband level, this translates to roughly **68 Mbps of aggregate capacity per subscriber** (700,000 Gbps ÷ 10.3M subscribers), though actual allocated capacity varies by spot beam, orbital position, and time of day. The disclosed median peak-hour speed of 225 Mbps suggests the network is not yet at full saturation in most markets.

**3. Headroom and Growth Trajectory**

The filing emphasizes that **capacity expansion—not congestion management—is SpaceX's primary strategy** for supporting subscriber growth:

- **V3 satellites** (deployment beginning H2 2026) are designed to deliver **1 Tbps each**, representing a step-change increase of roughly 12–13× over current V2 Mini satellites.
- A single **Starship launch** can deploy up to 60 V3 satellites, yielding ~60 Tbps per launch vs. ~3 Tbps per Falcon 9 V2 Mini launch—a **20× improvement in capacity deployed per launch**.
- For the mobile network, **V2 Mobile satellites** are planned for 2027 deployment, paired with EchoStar spectrum acquisition (65 MHz US + global MSS licenses), which the filing says will enable "orders of magnitude" increased mobile capacity.

**4. ARPU Trends Signal Market Mix Shift**

Starlink broadband ARPU declined from **$86 in Q1 2025 to $66 in Q1 2026**, driven by international expansion into lower-price markets. This suggests:
- New subscribers are increasingly concentrated in regions where per-user bandwidth demand may be lower (developing markets with less streaming/gaming traffic)
- The ~1,144 subscribers-per-satellite ratio may be sustainable in these markets but could create localized congestion in high-density, high-ARPU markets (e.g., North America, Europe) where bandwidth demands are higher
- SpaceX is effectively trading ARPU for volume, betting that capacity expansion via V3 will keep quality of service acceptable

**5. Key Risk: Saturation vs. Growth Timing**

The central utilization question is whether SpaceX can deploy V3 capacity fast enough to keep ahead of subscriber growth. With 10.3M broadband subscribers growing and ARPU declining, each satellite's effective revenue contribution is falling while user load is rising. The V3 transition in H2 2026 is the critical inflection point—if delayed, the 1,144:1 ratio could begin to manifest as degraded peak-hour performance in high-utilization cells.

---

### Summary

| Metric | Broadband | V1 Mobile |
|---|---|---|
| Users/Satellite | ~1,144 | ~11,385 |
| Service intensity per user | High (sustained broadband) | Low (intermittent, best-effort) |
| Capacity expansion path | V3 satellites (1 Tbps each, H2 2026) | V2 Mobile + EchoStar spectrum (2027) |
| Current saturation risk | Moderate (ARPU declining, user base growing) | Low (intermittent usage pattern) |

The V1 Mobile network's ~11,385 devices-per-satellite ratio appears sustainable given the low-bandwidth, intermittent nature of satellite-to-mobile connectivity. The broadband network's ~1,144 ratio is more operationally significant because of the sustained, high-throughput demands of each subscriber, but the disclosed 700+ Tbps aggregate capacity and median 225 Mbps peak speeds suggest the network is operating within comfortable margins ahead of the V3 deployment cycle.

**Sources:**
- SpaceX S-1 Filing (May 20, 2026): https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

- This is consistent with the V2 Mini satellite technology that comprises the majority of the current constellation

At the broadband level, this translates to roughly **68 Mbps of aggregate capacity per subscriber** (700,000 Gbps ÷ 10.3M subscribers), though actual allocated capacity varies by spot beam, orbital position, and time of day. The disclosed median peak-hour speed of 225 Mbps suggests the network is not yet at full saturation in most markets.

**3. Headroom and Growth Trajectory**

The filing emphasizes that **capacity expansion—not congestion management—is SpaceX's primary strategy** for supporting subscriber growth:

- **V3 satellites** (deployment beginning H2 2026) are designed to deliver **1 Tbps each**, representing a step-change increase of roughly 12–13× over current V2 Mini satellites.
- A single **Starship launch** can deploy up to 60 V3 satellites, yielding ~60 Tbps per launch vs. ~3 Tbps per Falcon 9 V2 Mini launch—a **20× improvement in capacity deployed per launch**.
- For the mobile network, **V2 Mobile satellites** are planned for 2027 deployment, paired with EchoStar spectrum acquisition (65 MHz US + global MSS licenses), which the filing says will enable "orders of magnitude" increased mobile capacity.

**4. ARPU Trends Signal Market Mix Shift**

Starlink broadband ARPU declined from **$86 in Q1 2025 to $66 in Q1 2026**, driven by international expansion into lower-price markets. This suggests:
- New subscribers are increasingly concentrated in regions where per-user bandwidth demand may be lower (developing markets with less streaming/gaming traffic)
- The ~1,144 subscribers-per-satellite ratio may be sustainable in these markets but could create localized congestion in high-density, high-ARPU markets (e.g., North America, Europe) where bandwidth demands are higher
- SpaceX is effectively trading ARPU for volume, betting that capacity expansion via V3 will keep quality of service acceptable

**5. Key Risk: Saturation vs. Growth Timing**

The central utilization question is whether SpaceX can deploy V3 capacity fast enough to keep ahead of subscriber growth. With 10.3M broadband subscribers growing and ARPU declining, each satellite's effective revenue contribution is falling while user load is rising. The V3 transition in H2 2026 is the critical inflection point—if delayed, the 1,144:1 ratio could begin to manifest as degraded peak-hour performance in high-utilization cells.

---

### Summary

| Metric | Broadband | V1 Mobile |
|---|---|---|
| Users/Satellite | ~1,144 | ~11,385 |
| Service intensity per user | High (sustained broadband) | Low (intermittent, best-effort) |
| Capacity expansion path | V3 satellites (1 Tbps each, H2 2026) | V2 Mobile + EchoStar spectrum (2027) |
| Current saturation risk | Moderate (ARPU declining, user base growing) | Low (intermittent usage pattern) |

The V1 Mobile network's ~11,385 devices-per-satellite ratio appears sustainable given the low-bandwidth, intermittent nature of satellite-to-mobile connectivity. The broadband network's ~1,144 ratio is more operationally significant because of the sustained, high-throughput demands of each subscriber, but the disclosed 700+ Tbps aggregate capacity and median 225 Mbps peak speeds suggest the network is operating within comfortable margins ahead of the V3 deployment cycle.

**Sources:**
- SpaceX S-1 Filing (May 20, 2026): https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm
